content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • •• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

July 9, 1976

Mr. Peter D. Bulens
City Attorney
City Hall - Third and H Streets
Post Office Box 369
Antioch, CA 94509

Dear Mr. Bulens:

This is in response to your letter of June 16, 1976, to
Mr. Goshorn of our staff requesting an informal opinion
concerning a possible conflict of interest on the part of a
certain city councilman.

Since your discussion of this matter with Mr. Goshorn,
the Commission approved the opinion regarding the City of
Davis by a four to zero vote (one member abstaining). The
result was the same as that reached in the draft we mailed
to you. Discussing the situation raised in your letter
subsequent to the aforementioned Commission action, the
staff concluded that the reasoning and holding of the final
portion of the City of Davis opinion (relating to Councilman
Black) is directly applicable. Based on the facts you have
provided, we do not believe that the Political Reform Act
would bar the Councilman in question from participating in
the budget vote of July 13; nor do we believe he would be
barred from making or participating in the making of decisions which affect his source of income. Only if the decisions foreseeably would have a demonstrably different or
specific impact on that one business would the Councilman be
required to disqualify himself.

Thank you for your letter. I trust this answers your
question.

Sincerely,

Michael Bennett
Executive Director

MB:pvp

ANTIOCH CA                94509

CITY HALL THIRD AND H   PO 369

June 16, 1976


Mr. Kenneth W. Goshorn
Staff Attorney
Fair Political Practices Commission
Conflicts of Interest Division
1100 "K" Street
Sacramento, California  95814

In re Request for Informal Opinion of Possible
Conflict of Interest

Dear Mr. Goshorn:

     I appreciate the draft opinion you sent to me last
week regarding the City of Davis.  Pursuant to our telephone
conversation of last week, I am hereby submitting my request
for an informal opinion regarding a possible conflict of
interest between a councilman and member of the Board of
the Antioch Development Agency and his interest in a business
within the boundaries of said development agency.  The facts
involved in this case are as follows:

     1.  The councilman in question has operated a men's
shop in Antioch for many years.  This business is incorporated
and the councilman owned a majority of the shares of said cor-
poration.  The present address of the business is now within
the Antioch Development Agency's boundaries.

     2.  Some few months ago the owner of the business was
elected as a councilman for the City of Antioch.  As a council-
man for the City of Antioch, he also serves as a member of the
Board of Directors for the Antioch Development Agency, a duly
authorized and existing redevelopment agency under the Health
and Safety Code of the State of California.  Said agency was
formed two (2) years ago.

     3.  Some three and one-half years ago the councilman
in question sold his interest in the men's shop to his son-in
law by a verbal contract.  Under the terms and conditions of
this verbal contract, the son-in-law is to pay to the council-
man the sum of one thousand dollars per month until his

Kenneth W. Goshorn
June 16, 1976
Page Two


obligation has been paid.  Such payments have been regularly
made for three and one-half years and still have some time
left.

     4.  The property upon which the men's shop is located
is not owned by the councilman or his son-in-law or the cor-
poration which is the men's shop, but rather it is owned by
a separate property owner who leases the property to the men's
shop.

     5.  The councilman in question is most desirous to
know whether he may legally participate in any votes regarding
the Antioch Development Agency.  Most particularly, we are
interested in his legal status regarding a vote concerning
the adoption of a budget for the forthcoming fiscal year and
any vote for the disbandment or continuation of the Antioch
Development Agency.  The present proposed budget for the
Antioch Development Agency involves certain street improve-
ments, including the signalization of an interchange, which
are not in close proximity to the men's shop in question and
in fact, are located several miles away.

     This problem is rather pressing at the moment, and
your kind consideration of this matter at the earliest possible
time would be appreciated.  The City Council has scheduled
another meeting of the Antioch Development Agency for July 13,
1976.  If your answer will be delayed after that date, I would
appreciate a call.

                                        Sincerely



                                        PETER D. BULENS
                                        City Attorney

PDB/bb

cc:  City Manager

sections.conclusion: Based on the facts you have provided, we do not believe that the Political Reform Act would bar the Councilman in question from participating in the budget vote of July 13, nor do we believe he would be barred from making or participating in the making of decisions which affect his source of income. Only if the decisions foreseeably would have a demonstrably different or specific impact on that one business would the Councilman be required to disqualify himself.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether a councilman with a continuing financial interest in a business within a redevelopment agency's boundaries may participate in agency votes, specifically regarding budget adoption and agency continuation.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concluded that the councilman may participate in the budget vote and other agency decisions affecting his income source, unless the decisions would have a demonstrably different or specific impact on his particular business. The reasoning from the City of Davis opinion regarding Councilman Black was found to be directly applicable.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: Document includes both the incoming request (June 16, 1976 letter from Peter D. Bulens) and the FPPC response (July 9, 1976 letter from Michael Bennett, Executive Director). The response references the City of Davis opinion and applies its reasoning. High confidence in extraction; OCR quality is generally good with minor formatting issues.

embedding.summary: In response to an inquiry from the City Attorney of Antioch, the FPPC provided an informal opinion regarding whether a city councilman with a financial interest in a business within a redevelopment agency's boundaries could participate in agency votes. The FPPC determined that the councilman could participate in budget votes and other decisions unless they had a demonstrably different impact on his specific business.
